sentences.text: No.
No, this was just related to me by the person who interviewed me for that first tape-- called me a few years later.
And she was somewhat amazed when a colleague of hers called her from the east coast.
I think they communicate on a regular basis.
And they probably discuss certain aspects of stories coming from the children.
And so that came up.
And so she remembered my explanation of that.
And she called me.
So I was kind of-- kind of vindicated because you sometimes wonder, you know, did I see it someplace?
Or did I read it or something?
Or was it a dream or not, so-- It sounds like it would be more of a nightmare.
[LAUGHS] Yeah, I don't-- you know, I can't drudge up the same emotions I felt at the time.
I don't really recall.
But I'm certain, being seven or eight years old, I'm sure I wasn't very comfortable, you know?
I don't recall.
I don't recall.
I don't think there was very much.
Other than those few isolated instances, I don't really recall anything.
Consciously, I don't really recall very much.
I'm not quite sure what I would recall if I were to be put on Demerol or something.
I'm not quite sure.
I don't think I suffered.
Personally, I don't think I suffered a lot of tragedies, which would force me to suppress information.
I think it was probably just childhood, you know, memory lapses.
I think--
Yeah.
Yeah, that may be.
But I think my mother prepared me for it.
You know, I did not come from a very loving home where there was a lot of Judaism, and where I was pulled away from my family and separated.
The circumstances that my mother faced prior to us going to the camp forced us already to be in a very-- in a position of being independent of each other.
And the-- and she had no choice, you know?
Otherwise she would have starved too.
So it probably wasn't as traumatic for me-- traumatic to me-- as it might have been for somebody else.
But actually the next-- the next thing I remember that was probably the most joyous thing I remember is the Russians coming.
And that was in early May.
I think we've heard some rumors before that, that things were happening in our favor, that the war was over, that the Allies had landed or something.
But we were on the Eastern Front.
So the Soviets actually came in and liberated the camp.
And I do remember the tanks rolling down the streets and, and passing by us.
Yes.
Well, we could see-- we could see the road.
We could see, you know, the roads where the main transports would be, where the main highway, or the main-- I wouldn't call it a highway-- but the main thoroughfare was going.
And that's where the Russians came in.
We saw them.
I mean we were behind-- we were behind barbed wires, but we saw them very clearly, heard them.
And of course there was a lot of cheering and weeping and so forth going on.
And I remember the Russians did throw us some food, whatever it was, some rations, just as a goodwill gesture.
And the Russians, I will-- to their credit, they maintained order.
They did not open up the gates and let everybody rush out.
I think they came in and set up some kind of a orderly [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] program to let people go where they needed to go and to move them to other areas of the country.
I do remember one instance-- and I don't know if this happened after they came, just before they did.
But we were already aware that the Germans were losing a war, and many of the German guards were abandoning these camps.
And I remember sleeping one night, waking up in the middle of the night with hearing a lot of noise.
And I looked out the window, and I saw a bonfire.
And a whole group of Jewish inmates around there with sticks and pitchforks, whatever they had.
And they had found a German guard who had-- who they had located.
He probably didn't get a chance to get away.
And they had him running into that fire.
And every time he ran out of the fire they would beat him until-- just beat him.
And then they'd shove him back into the fire.
Until finally-- and it was fascinating.
I was just mesmerized by this whole thing.
I was probably watching it for maybe, seems like a long time-- may have been five, 10 minutes until finally he fell into the fire where he passed on.
And so that was his mistake, by not getting out.
And it was right during that time when we were being liberated.
And--
It was probably about that time, when most of the guards had either been captured or had escaped.
And there was probably some straggler, or somebody who was unable to get away, or they captured him.
So it was at that time that we basically were free of the German control.
And the Russians were then in control.
No.
Again a lot of what I'm hearing-- a lot of my recollection is sort of muddy.
And I don't know if I recalled it exactly at the time.
Was it my independent recollection at the time I was there?
Or did I hear about it later, and did I associate it with the time I was there?

But I know one recollection I had-- and again, I don't know at what point it was-- where there were rumors floating around that this camp was going to be liquidated, eliminated, whether it was going to be they were building gas chambers, or they were going to move us out.
It was probably somewhere about March, April when the Germans realized that the tide was turning.
And they really had to get rid of a lot of inmates.
And there was a rumor floating around.
I don't know if I heard about it later, or if I heard about it at the time.
And I, I don't know what the reaction was, but it was definitely information that was coming through that they were building gas chambers.
Or either building them or preparing for us for them.
But I don't recall how I felt at the time.
I mean, I remember hearing about this thing.
And I don't know if I-- if I felt close enough to associate myself with it at the time, at that point.
We may have been just so beaten down that, you know, it may just have been another phase of our lives.
I don't know.
But anyway, in May-- first week, second week of May.
I don't remember.
First week of May, I believe, is the Soviets came.
But it was pretty close.
I think it was pretty close that they came by in the time that the inevitable was supposed to happen to us.
I think it was just a matter of weeks.
No, no I personally didn't.
If I did, I don't recall.
So anyway, then the Soviets came.
I don't recall very much after that.
We-- my mother and I-- went to Berlin at the time.
She had-- she wasn't quite sure what she was going to do.
But anyway, her-- just go back to Berlin to the city where we found some apartment.
I don't recall who it was with.
I don't know how she managed to pay them.
I don't know if she knew those people.

But I remember staying in an apartment, a one room apartment, right down the street from the-- from an American-- not the headquarters-- but from an American administrative office where there were a lot of American soldiers housed.
I remember seeing American flags there and remember a lot of American soldiers.
And my mother at that time had began a relationship with my stepfather, who was-- who was going to become a stepfather just very shortly thereafter.
And I remember following their steps all-- I remember Berlin.
I remember Berlin was just a rubbles.
I remember wandering the streets.
Of course there was no school.
I was, at that time I was nine in the summer of '45, looking at Berlin and all the rubbles, and not seeing very many buildings up, and just wandering the streets.
I think I had-- I had probably taken up company with a couple of other kids my age, and we would just wander the streets.
And we would follow the American soldiers.
And they were smoking cigarettes.
And they would flip the cigarettes and we would, we weren't smoking ourselves.
But we would gather all these cigarettes all day long.
And we would bring them home, and our parents would take these butts, and they would open them up and have a little tobacco so they could make their own cigarettes.
That was the pleasure of their lives.
I remember also where the building next door, or down the street, where the Americans had administrative offices, they would have all these coffee pots.
And they would just dump their coffee grinds in these trash cans.
And we would go in very carefully.
We would scrape off some of the coffee grinds, careful not to get anything else in there.
And we would put them in little bags, take them home.
And of course my parents would-- our parents would sort of re-grind this coffee 15 times.
Things of that nature.
I don't know how long that was-- six months, a year.
And then I believe my mother, at the time-- and I don't know if my stepfather was there.
I don't know if they had gotten married at that time.
He was-- she had met him in a camp in Theresienstadt.
At the time, he was a work party.
And he would go off with a work party to different camps.
And he was lib-- he was at Buchenwald.
He spent time in Buchenwald and Bergen-Belsen.
No, no, no, as an inmate.
He was Jewish.
No, no, no, he was also a German Jew.
Oh no, no.
No, they had taken-- she had-- excuse me.
I'll rephrase.
They had met in Theresienstadt.
He was out of a work party.
He came through as a work party.
But he had also been in Buchenwald and Bergen-Belsen.
But he had survived.
He was a fairly strapping man.
And he had come to Theresienstadt as a work party.
And that's where they had met-- and you know, man, woman thing.
And then when we were liberated, he was already someplace else.
But they had established enough of a relationship where they had wanted to connect again.
And I don't recall.
She tells me later on in life that she actually had to locate him.
And he was in another-- he was down south.
He had wound up down in southern Germany near this DP camp.
And for some-- you know, there was quite a network going on with, with bulletin boards and whatever, word of mouth, of have you seen so-and-so?
Have you seen so-and-so?
So by sheer luck and tenacity, she was able to locate him.
And so she had located him.

Then I don't know the sequence of events, but at that time she had found out that, that the HIAS organization then would take in the Jews and give them an option of where they would want to send the Jews, either to Israel, or to America, or back to Germany and so forth.
What organization?
The HIAS.
You know, it's a Jewish relief agency.
And H-I-A-S.
Hebrew Immigration Associations Society, something like that.
They're still around, I think.
I don't know if they changed their name or not.

But one of the cards that I'm unable to locate right now, an identity card, actually is from the HIAS group, which had my picture on it and my stamp on it which allowed me to board the ship to come to America.
So anyway by-- we wound up in the  DP camp in Deggendorf in about 1946.
With your--
With my mother and my stepfather.
At that time they had gotten married.
Max, Max, M-A-X Kurlander, K-U-R-L-A-N-D-E-R.
He was born in Breslau in the eastern part of Germany.
And he came from a very, very well-to-do family.
He-- they actually had sent him, as a young man, to Czechoslovakia.
He spoke a lot of languages.
And they had sent him to Czechoslovakia to become a tutor to other-- to wealthy Jews.
And he traveled extensively.
And at the time that he was over there, the winds of war were-- if I can steal Herman Wouks title-- were already adrift.
And his family back in Breslau gathered all of their family, and they moved.
They left Europe.
He didn't want to go, for some reason or other.
He chose to remain.
But his father and three brothers and family all moved to South America.
And he never saw them again.
As far as we know, they're still there.
That family's still there.
But he's never made contact with them again.
So he stayed in Europe, stayed in Germany-- actually Czechoslovakia, then came back to Germany.
And that's where he actually was taken.
And then subsequently, a number of years later, he met my mother in one of the camps.
And so they reconnected again in 1945, in late of 1945, 1946.
Connected, went to the DP camp in outside of Munich.
We stayed there for a couple of years until August of 1947, at what point we were able to come to America.
Yes, it was pretty bad.
The DP camp was also a sort of a-- it may have been a barracks at one time, a soldiers' barracks.
And it was used to, to house immigrants.
Immigrants, you know know, homeless, stateless, persons.
And my mother, at that time, thought that I should start a formal schooling.
So she sent me to a local grade school outside of the DP camp.
It was called-- I don't remember the school, but the name of the town was Deggendorf, which was a few kilometers south of Munich.
And the sheer stupidity of sending a Jewish kid to an all goyam school in Munich, outside of Munich, I don't know where her head was at.
But we just came from this whole horrible experience, and she sent me to school.
And obviously I was Jewish.
They knew exactly the first day I walked in who I was.
I mean, it's not like I was an observant Jew and I had-- was wearing a kippah.
But they knew I was Jewish.
And for every day I would get beat up by all these kids.
They just-- these kids my age would just gang up on me.
And I would come home every day bloodied.
And I tried to convince my mother that this was not a place for me.
Besides not learning anything, I was scared to death to go into that school.
And she kept sending me, kept sending me to that school.
Whether she wanted to get me out of my hair, but there was no schooling for kids in that camp, in that DP camp, in that displaced person camp.
It was basically-- it wasn't set up for that.
It was sort of like a way station.
So every parent had to make the decision on their own of what they were going to do with their school age children.
So she decided to send me to school.
Maybe she wanted to just get me out of her hair or thought I was going to get an education, or what.
But this lasted-- well, then of course I started playing hooky.
I mean I just wouldn't go.
And then she found out, and she would beat me.
So it was a matter of either her beating me, or get beat up by those kids.
[LAUGHS] And so finally, whatever, I think she finally realized it was futile to send me over there.
So I really never did get much of an education.
So when I was just about 11 years old we finally got onto one of the transport ships from Brahman and steerage, steerage passage to America.
Could you read at this point?
Oh, I'm sure I could.
I'm sure I could.
I'm sure I had basic rudimentary, you know-- I wasn't illiterate.
You know I wasn't well educated, but I wasn't illiterate.
I'm sure I could read, and--
I don't recall.
Probably very simple math.
I think there was no reason for me to use math.
But I'm sure I could use my, my basic-- you know, multiplication tables, I'm sure I had that pat down.
I'm sure I could read.
And I knew I could probably write to some degree.
Maybe not at a nine-year-old-- not at an 11-year-old level, but probably at a eight or nine-year-old level, I believe.
No.
Nothing.
There really wasn't very much to read.
I think we didn't have very much reading material.
But I did recall having to do certain things for my mother while we-- before the camp, when I was four or five.
I mean I did have some-- she was tutoring me, and I did have some lessons.
So I did have some basic educational material to follow.
I think I could.
I'm sure I could.
In German, don't forget, German.
Yeah, German.
So--
I remember eating like a pig.
I shouldn't say that, you know, God forbid, I shouldn't say eating like a pig.
But the food was well.
Yes.
The first was good.
I remember eating and getting sick, and eating and getting sick.
I think it was probably the first time in my memory that I had milk or cakes or something.
Even though we had food in the DP camp, it was still very basic.
And it was probably still soup and potatoes, and you know, bread.
So we were able to eat.
I-- the trip must have lasted six, seven days.
Seasick, yes.
I would still eat.
And I remember seeing the Statue of Liberty.
I remember the emotions of arriving in America.
Well, we were standing up on the deck.
We knew the Statute of Liberty.
I mean, we knew that this was America.
And of course it was on our mind for many years, at least during the DP camp.
Once we got to the DP camp, we had already-- my parents already decided that America was the place to go.
They did not choose Israel.
Although we had the opportunity to go.
But it was their decision.
I didn't have a choice in that.
No.
No, no.
No.
He was America.
I mean, it was America, first, second, and foremost.
Yeah, he spoke English.
He spoke about seven or eight languages.
And he, he spoke English, too.
No, she spoke-- German was her only language.
And German was my only language.
I didn't speak some Czech, because there were a lot of Czechoslovakians in the camp with us.
And so I was told that I spoke to Czech years later.
But I don't recall it, and I obviously forgot a lot of-- I obviously don't recall any of it.
So but I remember being on-- coming into Ellis Island, unloading and going through the sitting, and going through the processing, and you know, stamping us.
Suitcase.
No, no.
No, I think we landed.
And I think they gave us, each person $14 or $15.
They took us and they allowed us-- I think the HIAS had a-- had a building that we could stay in for a few days until we found a place to live.
I mean, they obviously didn't want to dump us on the docks and say you're on your own.
So they were able to get us to some place down the lower east side.
It was like an apartment building that they had taken over.
And they would-- the HIAS group would allow the immigrants to stay there for whatever time it took us to find an apartment.
And usually they encouraged us to do it very quickly.
So I don't think we were there for days, maybe a week or so.
But I did-- and I spoke no English-- but I did recall one day I was-- I mean, obviously there was no school.
It was in August.
School was not in session.
I remember sort of wandering around the street, and somebody comes up to me and said, do you want to go to a summer camp?
And I didn't understand what the English was.
But anyway, it was translated for me, and I said sure.
My mother said fine.
It turned out to be Eddie Cantor's Surprise Lake Camp.
And they were very active in, the the immigrant's children, of bringing them into camp-- a much better camp than we came from.
So I remember probably maybe two weeks after we arrived-- we came into America on August 3rd, 1947.
And in between August 3rd, and probably the first week in September, when school started, there was still summer camp in session.
So I was-- they shipped me up to upstate New York for a summer camp session, which was very nice.
I spoke no English.
But everybody was very nice.
It was Jewish kids, obviously.
And you know, living in a-- swimming in the lake and doing what kids do in a camp and then coming back.
Well yeah, they explained to us.
They explained what it was.
Oh yeah, yeah.
I think it was somebody came into the-- you know, must have been sitting on the stoop.
They knew who I was.
They said would like to go to summer camp?
I said well, you've got to speak to my mother or somebody.
And they explained to her, and she said, great.
You know, get me out of her hair.
I think with her it's just get rid of me, you know.
[LAUGHS] I was not a great joy of her life.
But although it was fine.
I mean, I'm sure she didn't-- I don't mean to speak ill of her.
But it's just that in retrospect, I don't know how.

I get to thinking that a lot of mothers wouldn't want their kid out of their hair-- would not want, after all this, all of a sudden, they would take a kid, go upstate New York, who knows who these people are.
We'll take you to another little camp, you won't see the kid for another two weeks.
I think that lot of Jewish mothers wouldn't have done it.
They would have wanted to keep their kids close by, and close knit family, and all that.
I believe a lot of-- Joan's mother, Joan, my wife, would not have done that.
If it had been her in the same situation, she'd have said no, you're not taking my kid.
So although it turned out to be a very positive experience, I think she-- it was not much of a decision for her to say, go.
So anyway, I went up there.
It was a nice positive experience.
I remember the lake and the trees, and upstate New York.
I didn't meet Eddie Cantor, but it was his camp.
But would you have known who he was if you did?
No, no idea.
I had no idea.
But he sponsored it, and it was a very famous thing.
And then we came back.

And when we came back my mother had already-- my mother and stepfather at that time had already found a-- an apartment down in the Lower East Side, Clinton Street and Houston Street, which was right in the heart of, of the Jewish-- I mean the Yiddish theater's there and the whole nine yards of what Jewish life really was.
And so that's where they settled in for a number of years and started their life in America.
Well, I went to school.
Went to a-- to a local grade school there.
Now I was 11 years old.
Never had any formal schooling.
Well, actually I didn't go unwillingly.
I was pretty flexible.
And so I went.
You know, I-- you know, I was raised in a very strict, authoritarian manner.
Even though I was independent and somewhat of a loner, I did follow the, the instructions.
And my mother says go to school, I went to school.
Probably, but I don't recall.
But I did know that we were living in a Jewish neighborhood.
So, but it's a good question.
That question has never been asked of me.
And I don't recall if I did.
But if I did, that fear was alleviated fairly quickly.
As soon as they knew I couldn't speak any English-- maybe two or three weeks, I might have been able to communicate a little better, but not enough to sit in a classroom.
And those were the days before English as a second language.
So they did-- the school assigned a teenage girl to me who spoke German, who was obviously living in the neighborhood from a German family, but who spoke English.
And I just sort of was in her tutelage for a couple of months.
So she would help me out, help me learn English, help me get acclimated to the school, and help me.
And then-- She met with you one-on-one?
One-on-one, right.
And after school, and in class and so forth.
I think she must have been two or three years older than I was.
I don't-- I don't recall her face, her name I don't-- I don't even recall her name because it didn't last that long.
But I know that-- I remember the concept that it happened.
